host-2013-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The 'Quality Buildings' passage names a 'catastrophic deterioration in weathering properties' from 20th-century reinforced concrete and synthetic cladding, then announces that 'our coatings technology is catching up' so today's new building no longer has to become tomorrow's eyesore. That arc — past mistake, current fix — matches C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What are we told here?' — broad, but you need the single takeaway of a four-sentence paragraph about building materials. Look for the structural shift the author signals.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two sentences carry the argument. The setup: 'the use of reinforced concrete and synthetic cladding in the 20th century led to a catastrophic deterioration in weathering properties.' The pivot: 'Now our coatings technology is catching up, and today's new building no longer has to be tomorrow's rust-streaked eye-sore.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Old materials (brick, stone) weathered well. The 20th century introduced reinforced concrete and synthetic cladding, which weathered badly. Today's coatings are fixing that problem — the industry has learned from those 20th-century mistakes.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Weathered' here means 'aged under exposure to weather' (rain, sun, frost). 'Reinforced concrete' = concrete strengthened with steel bars. 'Synthetic cladding' = man-made outer covering. 'Catastrophic deterioration' = very bad worsening. 'Coatings technology is catching up' = surface treatments are improving fast enough to solve the problem.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A claims future buildings will need similar repair to 20th-century ones — opposite of 'catching up'. B claims new materials resist extreme weather better than traditional ones — but the passage praises traditional brick/stone, not new materials. C ('learnt its lesson from the mistakes of the 20th century') maps directly onto the past-mistake / present-fix arc. D claims traditional materials will disappear — the passage never says that.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The paragraph is a two-beat structure: 20th-century materials failed, today's coatings are correcting that failure. C is the only option that names that lesson-learned trajectory.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read 'rust-streaked eye-sore' and assume future buildings will still need heavy repair. The grim phrase sticks in the mind.
distractors.why_wrong: The phrase describes what new buildings used to become; the very next clause says they 'no longer' have to. Step 5 catches it: 'no longer' flips the direction A claims.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'coatings technology is catching up' as 'new materials are better at handling weather', B feels close — the passage does talk about modern improvements.
distractors.why_wrong: The improvement is in coatings, not in the underlying materials, and the passage explicitly praises traditional materials ('brick and stone always weathered well'). Step 2 shows the contrast: traditional was good, 20th-century synthetics were bad, coatings now fix the synthetics.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'today's new building no longer has to be tomorrow's rust-streaked eye-sore' and infer that traditional materials are on the way out.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage opens by praising traditional materials for weathering well; it never says they will disappear. Step 5's match-against-options discipline catches the leap from 'today's coatings work' to 'traditional materials are obsolete'.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-031.technique: The past-mistake / present-fix arc: short ELF texts often pivot on one word ('Now', 'Today', 'Recently'). Find that pivot and the correct option is almost always the one that names the lesson learned.
host-2013-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: Vivid phrases like 'rust-streaked eye-sore' or 'catastrophic deterioration' tempt you to pick options that echo their gloom. The headline lives in the pivot sentence, not the imagery.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The 'Getting Physical' passage reports a study finding that 'the most active women had a 30 percent lower risk of cognitive decline' and concludes that 'even moderate levels of physical activity can serve to limit declines in cognition in older adults.' That ordinary-exercise-helps-older-minds claim is exactly B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is the main point here?' — so you need the single conclusion the paragraph is built to deliver, not any single study detail.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: The verdict sentence is the last: 'It seems that even moderate levels of physical activity can serve to limit declines in cognition in older adults.' Supporting fact: 'The most active women had a 30 percent lower risk of cognitive decline.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Researchers tracked active and inactive older women for 6–8 years. The more active ones held onto their thinking abilities better. The takeaway: even moderate, ordinary exercise (walking, stairs) helps older adults' brains.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Cognition' = thinking, mental processing. 'Cognitive decline' = age-related worsening of memory and thinking. 'Moderate levels of physical activity' = ordinary, everyday exercise — not athletic training. 'Underscored the link' = strengthened the connection (here, between activity and cognition).

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says active women 'improve their short-term memory' — the passage talks about cognitive decline broadly, not short-term memory specifically. B ('Ordinary exercise is beneficial for the intellectual functions of older people') is a clean paraphrase of the final sentence. C says women were assessed to evaluate their mental status — that's the method, not the point. D says mental health is unrelated to physical activity — the opposite of the finding.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The passage's headline is the closing sentence: moderate physical activity protects cognition in older adults. B states exactly that, in everyday wording.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading lands on 'researchers assessed their physical activity' and 'cognition' and you fill in a familiar phrase like 'short-term memory'.
distractors.why_wrong: The text never narrows the finding to short-term memory; the result is about cognitive decline broadly. Step 5 catches it — B uses the passage's actual scope, A invents a narrower one.

distractors.why_tempting: Most of the paragraph describes the METHOD: 5,925 women, four medical centres, questionnaires, follow-up assessments. It's easy to mistake the method for the point.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Main point' questions ask for the CONCLUSION, not the method. Step 1's framing keeps you anchored: the assessment was the tool, the cognitive-protection finding was the point.

distractors.why_tempting: If you skim and only catch 'cognitive impairment' and 'physical activity', a quick reader might assume the study found no link.
distractors.why_wrong: The finding is the opposite — 30 percent lower cognitive decline in the most active women. Step 3's paraphrase makes the direction clear: activity protects cognition, it doesn't fail to.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-032.technique: The verdict-sentence rule: paragraphs that describe research usually save their thesis for the last sentence. Read it first when the prompt asks for the main point — the rest is method, the closer is the message.
host-2013-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: Methodology paragraphs are dense with numbers (5,925 women, 33 activities, 6–8 years) that distract from the verdict. Don't let the impressive numbers pull you toward an option that names the procedure instead of the finding.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The 'Power of Music' passage stresses that 'music brings out predictable responses across cultures and among people of widely varying musical and cognitive abilities' and that 'music can reliably convey feelings' even in autistic listeners. Music understood the same regardless of origin — that is A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks which statement is 'most in line with the text'. That is a broad agreement check — you need an option the passage clearly supports, not one it merely mentions.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two anchors. On cross-cultural reach: 'music brings out predictable responses across cultures and among people of widely varying musical and cognitive abilities.' On listener experience: 'what we feel when we hear a piece of music is remarkably similar to what everybody in the room is experiencing.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Across cultures, across different cognitive profiles, music produces similar emotional responses in listeners. People who hear the same piece tend to feel the same thing. So the way music is understood emotionally barely depends on where the listener is from.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Elicit' = bring out, cause. 'Sentiment' = feeling, emotion. 'Convey' = communicate, carry. 'Predictable responses' = reactions that can be expected in advance. 'Widely varying musical and cognitive abilities' = a broad range of musical skill and thinking ability.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('Music is understood in much the same way regardless of people's origins') maps directly onto 'predictable responses across cultures'. B says music has been viewed as religious-spiritual healing — the passage lists 'gift of the heavens' and 'tool of the Devil' as past labels, but that isn't the main thrust. C claims music and mathematics trigger the same emotions — the passage mentions music as 'an extension of mathematics' as a historical label, not an emotional claim. D says music brings out unexpected reactions — the opposite of 'predictable responses'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The passage repeats the cross-cultural, cross-ability universality of emotional response to music. A states that universality in plain words.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to pick B after spotting 'gift of the heavens' and 'tool of the Devil' — those phrases sound religious-spiritual.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage lists those phrases as historical labels people once gave music, alongside 'extension of mathematics' and 'side effect of language processing'. B fixates on one label and reads it as the main view, but step 5 shows it isn't supported as a central claim.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'an extension of mathematics' and infer that math and music trigger similar emotions.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'extension of mathematics' label is again historical and is about description, not emotion. The passage never says mathematics triggers emotions in people — step 5 catches the leap from 'music has been compared to math' to 'they trigger the same emotions'.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a passage about new research is to assume the findings are surprising or unexpected.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly says 'predictable responses' and 'remarkably similar' reactions across listeners. D contradicts the central claim — step 2's anchor sentences settle it.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-033.technique: Agreement-check items ('most in line with the text'): find a sentence in the passage that is repeated in different words — universality phrasing here appears in three forms (across cultures, across abilities, same-room similarity). Whichever option restates that repeated idea in plain English wins.
host-2013-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: When a passage lists several historical labels for an idea ('gift of the heavens', 'tool of the Devil', 'extension of mathematics'), distractors pick one label and present it as the author's view. The author's view is in the framing sentences, not in any single label they mention.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The 'Power of Music' passage notes that 'music has been found to excite brain regions involved in understanding and producing language, including Broca's area and Wernicke's area'. The same brain region (Broca's) handles both music and language — that is C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is said about music and language?' — so you need an explicit claim the passage makes about how music and language relate.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: The relevant passage: 'music has been found to excite brain regions involved in understanding and producing language, including Broca's area and Wernicke's area, both located on the left hemisphere on the surface of the brain. Thus, musical syntax — for instance, the order of chords in a phrase — could have risen from the mechanisms that evolved to organize and understand grammar.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Listening to music activates the same brain areas that handle language — Broca's area and Wernicke's area. Music and language may share underlying brain machinery, so the brain treats musical structure a bit like grammar.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Broca's area' = a region in the left frontal lobe long associated with speech production. 'Wernicke's area' = a region in the left temporal lobe long associated with language understanding. 'Musical syntax' = the structural rules of music (chord order, phrase structure). 'Hijack brain systems' = use brain machinery that originally evolved for something else.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A claims research found no connection between language and music — the opposite of what the passage says. B says you need grammar skills to compose music — the passage compares STRUCTURES, not skills required. C ('Broca's area is used for experiencing music and using language') is the literal claim of step 2's sentence. D says musical skills are 'far more complex than language skills' — never stated.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. Broca's area shows up as a shared brain region for both music and language. C names that shared region in plain English.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read the passage as a celebration of music's uniqueness, you might assume music and language are kept separate in the brain.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage's central neuroscience claim is the opposite: music excites the same brain regions as language. Step 2's quote settles it — Broca's and Wernicke's are language regions and they're also music regions.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'musical syntax… could have risen from the mechanisms that evolved to organize and understand grammar' as 'you need grammar to compose music'.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage proposes a shared evolutionary origin between musical and grammatical structure — not a practical requirement that composers must know grammar. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the level of claim correct: STRUCTURES are similar, not SKILLS required.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the parade of brain regions and varied effects (emotion, movement, language) and infer that music is more complex than language.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage never compares the complexity of musical and language skills. It only says they share brain machinery. Step 5's discipline catches the unsupported comparison.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-034.technique: Anatomy-naming sentences are gold for ELF: when a passage names a specific brain area, person, or year, scan the options for the one that quotes that anatomy/person/date back to you. The right answer rewards close reading of one sentence.
host-2013-verb1-ELF-034.pitfall: Speculative phrases like 'could have risen from' or 'may have' get rephrased by distractors as firm requirements ('grammar skills ARE needed'). Notice the modal verbs — they constrain the level of claim the passage actually makes.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: The 'Power of Music' passage reports a study with autistic and non-autistic children matching music to emotions: 'kids' ability to recognize different feelings in music did not depend on their diagnosis'. Autistic children can tell apart feelings in music — that is C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What has been found in research on music in relation to autistic people?' — you need a specific finding from the autism-related study described in the passage.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: The study description: 'a cognitive research team played music for both autistic and non-autistic children, comparing those with similar language skills, and asked the kids to match the music to emotions. They found that kids' ability to recognize different feelings in music did not depend on their diagnosis. Autistic and typical children with similar verbal skills performed equally well…'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Researchers played music to autistic and non-autistic children with similar verbal skills, then asked them to match each piece to an emotion. The autistic group did just as well as the non-autistic group. So autistic children CAN tell different feelings apart in music.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Autistic' = on the autism spectrum, typically with social-communication differences. 'Verbal skills' = language abilities. 'Did not depend on their diagnosis' = the result was the same whether or not the child was autistic. 'Emotion-laden social cues' = signals that carry emotional meaning (facial expressions, tone of voice).

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says autistic children aren't emotionally touched by music — contradicts the finding. B says research found no difference in how autistic and non-autistic people PERFORM music — the study is about RECOGNISING emotions in music, not performing it. C ('Autistic children can distinguish between various feelings when listening to music') is a clean paraphrase of step 2's quote. D says autistic people 'in many ways benefit from listening to music' — the passage doesn't claim broad benefits, only that recognition is intact.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The study's finding is specifically that autistic children CAN distinguish feelings in music as well as non-autistic peers. C states exactly that.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading might land on the phrase 'people whose ability to pick up emotion-laden social cues, such as facial expressions or tone of voice, is severely compromised' and you infer autistic people aren't touched by music either.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage uses that phrase to highlight the CONTRAST: even people who struggle to read facial cues CAN still feel emotion in music. Step 2's anchor settles it — the result is intact recognition, not impaired emotional response.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to swap 'recognise feelings' for 'perform music' — both involve musical skill.
distractors.why_wrong: The study had children MATCH music to emotions (a perception task), not play instruments (a performance task). Step 5 catches the swap — the passage doesn't say anything about autistic and non-autistic performance.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'music's ability to convey feelings may underlie one of its most important benefits' as benefits for autistic listeners specifically, D feels close.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'most important benefits' sentence is about music's general social function (communal events, group cohesion), not about therapeutic benefits for autistic individuals. Step 3's paraphrase stays anchored to the specific autism finding: feelings are recognised, not that benefits accrue.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-035.technique: Research-finding items: lock onto the actual MEASURE the study used ('match music to emotions') and the actual RESULT ('did not depend on diagnosis'). The right option restates measure + result; distractors swap one for an adjacent idea (perform vs. perceive; recognise vs. benefit).
host-2013-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: When a passage's broader argument has a positive frame about a group, distractors generalise the positive frame ('autistic people benefit broadly') into something the study didn't actually show. Keep the option scoped to what the study measured.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: The 'Power of Music' passage explains that upbeat, tense music 'can physically excite the listener, triggering the body's fight and flight response: heart and breathing rates increase'. This 'pumping up' explains why people enjoy rock during workouts — the music 'primes the psychological systems needed for high-energy movement'. That is B: it activates the body's basic drive system.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks WHY fast modern music is popular among people who exercise. You need the specific physiological / psychological reason the passage gives, not a personality claim.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: The relevant passage: 'upbeat, tense or exciting music can physically excite the listener, triggering the body's fight and flight response: heart and breathing rates increase, a person may break out in a sweat, and adrenaline enters the blood-stream. This 'pumping up' effect explains why so many people enjoy listening to rock while they work out — the music primes the psychological systems needed for high-energy movement.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Fast, exciting music turns on the body's basic stress / arousal response — heart rate up, breathing up, adrenaline up. That is exactly the body state you need for hard exercise. So people pick rock to put themselves in that state.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Upbeat' = lively, cheerful (also fast-tempo). 'Fight and flight response' = the body's automatic preparation for danger — heart racing, muscles ready. 'Pumping up' = energising, charging up. 'Primes' = prepares (a system) for action. 'Psychological systems' here means brain-body systems for arousal and motivation.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says exercisers are too impatient for classical music — the passage never makes that personality claim. B ('This kind of music activates the brain's basic drive system') is a clean paraphrase of 'triggering the body's fight and flight response… primes the psychological systems'. C says exercisers want to seem young — never mentioned. D says fast music reduces pain — the pain-reduction claim in the passage applies to CALMING music, not fast exciting music.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The passage explains the appeal of fast music for exercisers through one mechanism: it switches on the body's basic arousal system. B names that mechanism in plain words.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to project a personality stereotype — gym-goers are impatient, classical is slow — onto the question.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage never characterises exercisers' personalities at all. The 'why' is purely physiological (arousal system). Step 5 keeps you on what the text says, not on outside assumptions.

distractors.why_tempting: Rock-while-exercising is culturally tied to youth, so 'young at heart' feels like a thematic match.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage gives a body-science reason (fight-or-flight activation), not an identity reason. Step 2's quote shows the mechanism — adrenaline, heart rate, breathing — not self-image.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the later mention of music 'alleviating pain' and pair it with the exercise context (sore muscles, pushing through pain).
distractors.why_wrong: The pain-alleviation finding is attributed to CALMING music ('music can be calming, reducing… cortisol… and alleviating pain'), not to fast upbeat rock. Step 5 catches the mix-up: two different music types have two different effects.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-036.technique: Cause-question items ('Why, according to the text…'): the answer is the mechanism in the passage, not a plausible-sounding cultural explanation. Look for body-or-brain language ('arousal', 'response', 'system') and match it to the option that names the same mechanism.
host-2013-verb1-ELF-036.pitfall: When a passage discusses two opposing effects of the same broad topic (exciting music vs. calming music here), distractors swap effects between the two. Tag which effect belongs to which type before scanning options.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: The 'Power of Music' passage closes by listing several health benefits of calming music — 'reducing the levels of the stress hormone cortisol in the blood, lowering heart and respiration rates, and alleviating pain'. Music has been found to help several health problems — that is D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what the text says about the MEDICAL EFFECTS of music. You need an option that captures what the passage claims about music and health, in scope and tone.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: The final part of the passage: 'music can be calming, reducing the levels of the stress hormone cortisol in the blood, lowering heart and respiration rates, and alleviating pain, according to several studies.' Earlier: 'Energizing melodies tend to boost our mood in general, waking us up if we are feeling tired and creating a sense of excitement…'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The passage lists several health-related effects of music: it can lower stress hormones, slow heart and breathing rates, ease pain, and lift mood. Different music types help in different ways. So music has positive effects on several health-related problems.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Medical effects' = effects on health and the body. 'Cortisol' = the body's main stress hormone. 'Respiration rate' = breathing rate. 'Alleviating pain' = reducing pain. 'According to several studies' = supported by more than one piece of research.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says only cheerful music has an effect when you're feeling down — too narrow; the passage describes upbeat AND calming music. B says music is a worthwhile alternative to medicine for heart problems — the passage never positions music as a replacement for medicine. C says all kinds of music have been used to eliminate stress — too sweeping; only calming music is linked to cortisol reduction. D ('Music has been found to have a positive impact on several health problems') exactly matches the passage's range of medical benefits.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The passage lists several distinct health benefits — stress hormones, heart rate, breathing rate, pain, mood. D captures that breadth without overclaiming.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'Energizing melodies tend to boost our mood in general, waking us up if we are feeling tired' as 'only cheerful music helps when you're down'.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes TWO modes — energising music for tiredness AND calming music for stress / pain. Step 5 catches the narrowing: A loses half of the passage's medical effects.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'lowering heart and respiration rates' and infer that music could replace medicine for heart conditions.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says music has effects on heart rate, not that it replaces cardiac medicine. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the claim at the right level — physiological effects, not therapy substitution.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a positive passage is to read it as universal — 'all kinds of music' for stress.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage attributes stress-reduction to CALMING music specifically, not all music. Energising music does the opposite — it raises heart rate and adrenaline. Step 5's option-by-option check catches the over-generalisation.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-037.technique: When a passage lists several specific benefits (cortisol, heart rate, breathing, pain), prefer the option that uses a covering word like 'several' or 'a range of'. Specific-but-correct beats sweeping ('all', 'only', 'always') — distractors love absolutes.
host-2013-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: Don't confuse 'medical effects' with 'replaces medicine'. ELF passages describe what music DOES to the body; distractors inflate that into a therapeutic substitution claim the passage doesn't make.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The 'In and Out of School' passage argues that 'improving their home environment is not the way' to improve classroom behaviour — 'what you need is a school-based intervention. That is where the teachers have power.' Only teachers can fix classroom behaviour — that is D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is said in the text?' — broad, but you need the specific claim the passage builds toward. Watch for the contrast the author sets up.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Three anchors. 'Children learn at home how to behave at home… and they learn outside the home how to behave outside the home.' 'Improving their home environment is not the way to do it.' 'What you need is a school-based intervention. That is where the teachers have power.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Kids learn home behaviour at home and school behaviour at school — the two settings are separate. So if you want to improve how kids behave in class, fixing the home doesn't help. You need a school-based fix, where teachers have the power.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Diligent' = hardworking. 'Disruptive' = causing disorder. 'Intervention' = a planned action to change a situation. 'Fractions' here (typo for 'factions') = opposing groups. 'School-based' = happening within the school, not at home.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says parents are responsible for unruly classroom behaviour — opposite of the passage's claim that home and school behaviour are learned separately. B says teachers and parents should cooperate — never argued; the passage explicitly separates the two domains. C says disruptive pupils should be sent to special classes — never mentioned. D ('Teachers are the only ones able to improve classroom management and pupils' behaviour') matches 'school-based intervention… that is where the teachers have power'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The passage's whole argument is that only school-based intervention by teachers can change classroom behaviour, because that is where teachers (not parents) have power. D states that conclusion directly.

distractors.why_tempting: If you skim the opening — 'children learn at home how to behave at home' — and stop there, you might infer that home conditions cause classroom behaviour, so parents are responsible.
distractors.why_wrong: The next sentence flips that direction: 'improving their home environment is not the way' to change classroom behaviour. Home behaviour and school behaviour are separated. Step 2's anchor settles it.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to assume any passage about behaviour will recommend the standard advice — parents and teachers cooperating.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage actively argues AGAINST that view. It says home interventions don't transfer to school behaviour, so cooperation across domains is beside the point. Step 5 catches the leap to a cooperative framing the passage doesn't endorse.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading might pick up 'making them more diligent and less disruptive in the classroom' and suggest separating disruptive pupils.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage proposes a SCHOOL-WIDE intervention by teachers, not separating disruptive pupils into other classes. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the proposal at the level of the whole class group, with the teacher leading.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-038.technique: The contrast-pivot pattern: when a passage sets up two settings (home / school here) and explicitly rules one out, the correct option is the one that names the remaining setting as the locus of power.
host-2013-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: Don't import common-sense expectations about parent-teacher cooperation onto a passage that argues the opposite. ELF questions are about what the text says, not what is sensible.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: The 'Religious Thoughts' passage notes that 'thinking about religion is no different from thinking about secular things — at least from the standpoint of the brain' and that evaluating the truth of either type of statement 'was found to involve the same regions of the brain.' Researchers reject the view that religious beliefs are different — that is A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is said about beliefs in connection with brain activity. You need the passage's specific claim about how the brain handles religious vs. non-religious beliefs.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: The passage in full: 'Religious belief may seem to be a unique psychological experience, but a growing body of research shows that thinking about religion is no different from thinking about secular things — at least from the standpoint of the brain. In the first imaging study to compare religious and non-religious thoughts, evaluating the truth of either type of statement was found to involve the same regions of the brain.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: People might assume religious thinking is special and unique. Brain imaging says no — the same brain regions process religious AND non-religious thoughts when you evaluate whether a statement is true. So the brain doesn't single out religious belief as different.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Unique' = one-of-a-kind. 'Psychological experience' = something that happens in your mind. 'Secular' = non-religious, worldly. 'From the standpoint of the brain' = as far as brain activity is concerned. 'Imaging study' = a study using brain scans (fMRI, PET).

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('Researchers reject the view that religious beliefs are different from other beliefs') matches the passage's claim that religious and secular thinking use the same brain regions. B says non-religious beliefs are processed the same regardless of gender — gender is never mentioned. C says researchers claim religion needs further investigation — never stated. D says religious beliefs leave special marks in the brain — the opposite of the finding.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The passage's claim is that brain imaging removes the supposed uniqueness of religious belief — the same regions process both kinds. A states that rejection in plain English.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'either type of statement' as covering different demographic groups, gender is a natural variable to read in.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage compares religious vs. secular statements, not men vs. women. Step 2's anchor restricts the comparison to belief TYPE, not believer demographics. Step 5 catches the off-topic dimension.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'a growing body of research' and infer that researchers are calling for more investigation.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't call for more research; it reports a finding. 'A growing body of research' means the evidence base is expanding, not that more is needed. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the focus on the result, not the call.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a passage about brain imaging and religion is to assume the finding shows something special about religious belief in the brain.
distractors.why_wrong: The actual finding is the opposite — no special religious signature in the brain; religious and secular thoughts share regions. Step 2's quote ('the same regions of the brain') is the corrective.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-039.technique: When a passage opens with 'X may seem unique, BUT…' you have a debunking structure. The correct option always names the debunking (here: religious belief is NOT brain-special), not the popular view being debunked.
host-2013-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: Don't import a hypothesis the passage debunks. Distractors love to restate the very view ('religious beliefs leave special marks') the passage rejects.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The 'Taxes' passage notes that thousands of taxpayers may be 'missing out on substantial refunds' because the system doesn't automatically send self-assessment forms to those most at risk (mid-year leavers, casual workers). The implied conclusion: the system is unfair and will need to be revised — that is D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is implied here?' — an implication, not an explicit statement. You need the conclusion the passage points toward without quite spelling out.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two anchors. 'Generally, people whose tax affairs are straightforward do not have to complete a self-assessment form, because there is no more tax to pay. However, thousands of taxpayers who have not received a form may be missing out on substantial refunds because they have paid too much tax.' Followed by the at-risk groups: mid-year leavers and casual / portfolio workers.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The current rule is: simple tax situation, no form. But two groups slip through that rule and overpay tax: people who stop work mid-year and people with several jobs at once. They don't get a form, so they miss out on refunds they're owed. The system is failing them — implicitly, it needs to change.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Self-assessment form' = a tax form taxpayers fill in themselves. 'Substantial refunds' = sizeable repayments. 'Apportioned' = divided up proportionally. 'Casual workers' = irregular, non-permanent workers. 'Portfolio workers' = people with several part-time jobs at once.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says taxpayers with small incomes don't need a form to be fairly taxed — the passage's whole point is that some DO need a form and don't get one. B says the system is unfair to all part-time workers — too broad; the passage specifies casual / portfolio workers, not all part-timers. C says people with multiple jobs WILL have part of their taxes repaid — the passage says they MAY be missing out on refunds, not that the repayment is automatic. D ('The present system will have to be revised to be fair to all taxpayers') is the implied conclusion: the system has a gap, so it needs revising.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The passage describes a flaw in the current rule, names who is hurt, and quantifies the cost ('substantial refunds'). The unspoken next step — system revision — is what D names.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to combine 'people whose tax affairs are straightforward do not have to complete a self-assessment form' with 'small income' as a synonym for 'straightforward'.
distractors.why_wrong: Small income and straightforward affairs are not the same — a mid-year leaver may have low overall income but a complicated apportionment situation. The passage's point is exactly that the simple rule misses these cases. Step 5 catches the substitution.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'casual or portfolio workers' as 'part-time workers in general', B feels close — the passage does say the present system hurts certain workers.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage names two specific at-risk groups: mid-year leavers AND casual / portfolio workers. B over-generalises to 'all part-time workers', which the passage doesn't claim. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the scope tight.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading hits 'thousands of taxpayers… may be missing out on substantial refunds' and 'casual or portfolio workers who might have several jobs simultaneously' and you infer multi-job workers automatically get refunds.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says they ARE missing out — they don't get the form, so they don't get the refund. C reverses the direction, treating a failure as an automatic success. Step 2's anchor settles it.

host-2013-verb1-ELF-040.technique: Implication-question items: when a passage describes a flaw without proposing a fix, the correct option usually names the unspoken fix at the system level ('will have to be revised'). Specifics-only options miss the implied step.
host-2013-verb1-ELF-040.pitfall: When a passage names specific at-risk groups (mid-year leavers, casual workers), distractors over-generalise ('all part-time workers') or substitute an adjacent group ('low-income taxpayers'). Keep the scope as tight as the passage does.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-031.solution_path: Gap 31 sits in 'it begins with very 31_____ symptoms. Tense muscles in an arm or shoulder, for example, tend to send people to orthopedists, not neurologists.' Early Parkinson's is mistaken for muscle problems — the symptoms must be MILD enough to look like something else. The answer is B, 'mild'.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: This is a gap-fill (cloze) — pick the ADJECTIVE that fits 'very 31_____ symptoms'. Use the context: early Parkinson's is often missed because the symptoms look like something else.

steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap
steps.text: 'But often the malady is not recognized soon enough, because it begins with very 31_____ symptoms. Tense muscles in an arm or shoulder, for example, tend to send people to orthopedists, not neurologists. Fatigue, depression or sudden outbreaks of sweating are typically pegged to other common problems.'

steps.title: What kind of adjective does the sentence need?
steps.text: The whole point is that the early symptoms are easy to MISTAKE — they look like ordinary muscle aches, tiredness, or sweating. So the adjective should mean 'not severe', 'easy to overlook', 'unremarkable'. Words like 'mild', 'subtle', 'vague' fit; 'severe' or 'rare' do not.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Mild' = not severe, gentle. 'Steady' = constant, unchanging. 'Soft' = not hard / not loud (rarely used for medical symptoms). 'Rare' = uncommon, infrequent. Of these, 'mild' is the standard medical word for symptoms that aren't severe.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Walk each option. A 'very steady symptoms' — steady means unchanging; that wouldn't explain why doctors miss them. B 'very mild symptoms' — fits perfectly; mild early symptoms are why people see the wrong specialist. C 'very soft symptoms' — 'soft' isn't a medical collocation; you don't say 'soft symptoms'. D 'very rare symptoms' — rarity wouldn't explain why the symptoms get attributed to common everyday problems; in fact, the passage says fatigue and depression are 'common'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. 'Very mild symptoms' is the standard collocation and explains exactly why early Parkinson's is misdiagnosed as orthopedic or general.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'tense muscles in an arm or shoulder' as a sustained low-grade tension, 'steady' might feel close.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Steady' means unchanging, not gentle — and it doesn't explain the misdiagnosis. The passage is specifically about symptoms being mild enough to mistake for something else. Step 3's analysis fixes the meaning the gap needs.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Soft' is a natural-sounding everyday adjective, and English learners may translate from a native-language collocation.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Soft' doesn't collocate with 'symptoms' in English medical usage. You can have 'soft tissue', 'soft voice', 'soft palate' — but symptoms are 'mild', not 'soft'. Step 4's gloss flags the collocation problem.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'is not recognized soon enough' and infer the symptoms must be unusual.
distractors.why_wrong: The next sentences say the opposite — the early symptoms (fatigue, depression, sweating) are 'pegged to other common problems'. They are COMMON-looking, not rare. Step 5's plug-and-test catches the contradiction.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-031.technique: On cloze adjectives, read TWO sentences past the gap — the author usually spells out the consequence (here: misdiagnosis), and that consequence fixes the missing adjective's meaning.
host-2013-verb2-ELF-031.pitfall: English-style collocations matter: 'mild symptoms' is the natural medical pairing, while 'soft symptoms' is grammatically possible but unnatural. Native-speaker rhythm rules out otherwise plausible words.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-032.solution_path: Gap 32 sits in 'nine to 12 years can pass before the disease 32_____ asserts itself. But by then a large percentage of cells in certain brain regions have already died.' The adverb modifies 'asserts itself' — the disease eventually shows itself completely. The answer is D, 'fully'.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: Cloze adverb — pick the adverb that modifies 'asserts itself'. The clue is in the time-frame: 9–12 years before the disease becomes fully visible.

steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap
steps.text: 'These conditions often remain mild for a long time; nine to 12 years can pass before the disease 32_____ asserts itself. But by then a large percentage of cells in certain brain regions have already died.'

steps.title: What kind of adverb does the sentence need?
steps.text: The contrast is 'mild for a long time' vs. eventually clear. The adverb needs to mean 'completely', 'in its full form'. The disease doesn't just kind-of show — it FULLY presents itself, finally. Words like 'fully', 'completely', 'clearly' fit; 'rapidly', 'temporarily', 'occasionally' do not.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Fully' = completely, entirely. 'Hardly' = barely, almost not. 'Vaguely' = indistinctly, unclearly. 'Suddenly' = abruptly, all at once. The verb 'asserts itself' means 'makes itself clearly known' — it pairs naturally with 'fully'.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Walk each option. A 'hardly asserts itself' — would mean the disease barely shows even after 12 years; that contradicts 'a large percentage of cells have already died'. B 'vaguely asserts itself' — same contradiction; vague signs after 12 years doesn't match the severity described. C 'suddenly asserts itself' — 'suddenly' clashes with 'nine to 12 years can pass' (a slow build, not a sudden onset). D 'fully asserts itself' — fits the slow build to full-blown disease.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. After 9–12 years of mild symptoms, the disease 'fully asserts itself' — becomes completely visible. The brain cell loss confirms the severity at that point.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'remain mild for a long time' as 'never really becomes severe', 'hardly' feels like a continuation of that mildness.
distractors.why_wrong: The sentence after the gap reports massive cell loss — 'a large percentage of cells in certain brain regions have already died'. That is severe disease, not 'hardly'. Step 5's plug-and-test catches the contradiction.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'mild for a long time' and assume the eventual presentation is also unclear.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Vaguely asserts itself' is internally contradictory — 'assert' means make clear, 'vaguely' means unclear. The passage describes a clear severe presentation. Step 4's gloss flags the clash.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'before the disease 32_____ asserts itself' is to read a transition word — and 'suddenly' is a familiar one for medical onset.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage frames the disease as building over 9–12 years, not appearing suddenly. 'Suddenly asserts itself' clashes with the slow-onset description. Step 3's analysis catches the timing mismatch.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-032.technique: On cloze adverbs, anchor on the VERB the adverb modifies. 'Assert itself' is a 'make clear' verb — it takes adverbs of degree and completeness ('fully', 'clearly'), not of speed or doubt.
host-2013-verb2-ELF-032.pitfall: Two-clause cloze sentences: read the clause AFTER the gap. Here, 'a large percentage of cells have already died' fixes the severity, which fixes the adverb's strength.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-033.solution_path: Gap 33 sits in 'everyday tasks such as combing hair, buttoning a shirt or tying shoes become impossible. Patients become 33_____ others, and their quality of life declines dramatically.' Once basic self-care is impossible, patients need others to do it for them — they become DEPENDENT ON others. The answer is C, 'dependent on'.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: Cloze adjective-phrase — pick what completes 'Patients become 33_____ others'. The clue: patients can no longer do everyday self-care.

steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap
steps.text: 'Individuals begin to lose their fine-motor coordination: they cannot thread a needle, and their handwriting becomes tiny and hard to read. Soon everyday tasks such as combing hair, buttoning a shirt or tying shoes become impossible. Patients become 33_____ others, and their quality of life declines dramatically.'

steps.title: What relationship does the phrase need?
steps.text: The patient cannot do basic self-care any more. They need somebody else to do those tasks. So the relationship is 'needs help from others' — i.e., dependent on others, reliant on others. The phrase pairs an adjective with the preposition 'on'.

steps.title: Vocabulary check (adjective + preposition)
steps.text: 'Distant from others' = emotionally / physically far. 'Different from others' = unlike others. 'Dependent on others' = needing help / support from others. 'Disliked by others' = others dislike them. The grammar matters: dependent ON, different FROM, disliked BY — and the sentence ends '33_____ others', taking whatever preposition fits.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Walk each option. A 'become distant from others' — the gap text ends with 'others', not 'from others'; and the passage isn't about emotional distance, it's about physical incapacity. B 'become different from others' — same preposition mismatch; and 'different' is too vague for the loss of self-care. C 'become dependent on others' — perfectly fits both the meaning (needing help with daily tasks) and the syntax ('dependent on others'). D 'become disliked by others' — the passage never says other people dislike the patients; this is irrelevant.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The previous sentence lists everyday tasks patients can no longer perform; the natural next step is becoming dependent on others to perform them.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to project an emotional consequence of disability — patients feel cut off from social life — onto the gap.
distractors.why_wrong: The sentence is about practical capacity, not emotional distance. The list right before — combing hair, buttoning a shirt, tying shoes — is concrete physical care, not social withdrawal. Step 3's analysis keeps the relationship physical.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Different from others' is a vague answer that loosely fits any change in health.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage already named the specific change (cannot do self-care). 'Different' adds no information about the relationship between the patient and 'others' — it doesn't explain who the others are or what they do. Step 4's gloss makes 'dependent ON' the precise fit.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading might leap from 'quality of life declines' to social consequences like dislike or rejection.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes the patient's own life and capacities; it never claims others dislike them. Step 5's plug-and-test catches the off-topic direction.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-033.technique: On cloze items with a preposition cue (the word after the gap, e.g. 'others'), check which option needs which preposition. 'Dependent on' uses 'on', 'different from' uses 'from'. The given preposition rules out half the options before meaning even matters.
host-2013-verb2-ELF-033.pitfall: Distinguish physical from emotional consequences: when a passage lists concrete capacities the patient loses, the missing word names the practical replacement (someone else helps), not an emotional state.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-034.solution_path: Gap 34 sits in 'Scientists have known since the late 1920s that 34_____ the disease advances, neurons in the midbrain die off.' The two clauses describe parallel processes: as one happens, the other happens. The conjunction is 'as' — temporal / parallel. The answer is B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: Cloze conjunction — pick the word that links 'the disease advances' to 'neurons in the midbrain die off'. Decide the relationship between the two clauses first.

steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap
steps.text: 'Scientists have known since the late 1920s that 34_____ the disease advances, neurons in the midbrain die off. Neuroscientists still cannot say definitively what triggers the trouble.'

steps.title: What relationship does the conjunction need?
steps.text: Two processes happen together over time: the disease worsens, and brain cells die. The two are parallel — one accompanies the other. The conjunction is temporal ('as', 'while', 'when'). It is not 'unless' (negative condition), not 'although' (contrast), and not 'why' (reason).

steps.title: Vocabulary check (conjunctions)
steps.text: 'Unless' = if not, except when (negative condition). 'As' = while, at the same time as (parallel time + cause). 'Although' = even though (contrast). 'Why' = the reason that (introduces a cause; usually after verbs like 'know', 'wonder').

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Walk each option. A 'unless the disease advances, neurons die off' — would mean neurons die ONLY IF the disease doesn't advance; nonsensical. B 'as the disease advances, neurons die off' — perfectly fits the parallel-time relationship. C 'although the disease advances, neurons die off' — sets up a contrast that isn't there; the two are aligned, not opposed. D 'why the disease advances, neurons die off' — ungrammatical in this position; 'why' would need 'know WHY' followed by a complete clause, not this structure.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The two processes (disease advance and neuron death) run in parallel. 'As' is the standard conjunction for parallel-time relations.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'Scientists have known' as a piece of conditional knowledge — what they know depends on circumstances — 'unless' might creep in.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Unless' introduces a negative condition: it would mean 'neurons die off only if the disease doesn't advance'. That is the opposite of what's intended. Step 5's plug-and-test catches the polarity flip.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Although' looks plausible because it links two clauses and feels neutral.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Although' marks a contrast — and there's no contrast here. Disease advance and neuron death go TOGETHER, not against each other. Step 3's analysis identifies the relationship as parallel, ruling out 'although'.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Scientists have known… that' and think 'why' continues a knowledge clause ('know why X happens').
distractors.why_wrong: If 'why' were correct, the sentence would parse as 'Scientists have known that why the disease advances, neurons die off' — broken syntax. 'Why' doesn't fit in a 'that-clause' as a conjunction. Step 4's gloss flags the grammar problem.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-034.technique: On cloze conjunctions, identify the LOGICAL relationship between the two clauses first (parallel time, contrast, condition, reason). Only then check which option fits — most conjunctions are unambiguous once the relationship is named.
host-2013-verb2-ELF-034.pitfall: Beware 'although' and 'unless' as default-ish answers: they look neutral and grammatically clean, but they impose a specific relationship (contrast / negative condition) that has to be actually present in the sentence.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-035.solution_path: Gap 35 sits in 'A genetic defect is 35_____ in five to ten percent of cases, and the aberrations provide interesting clues.' Genetic defects PLAY A ROLE in 5–10% of cases — the natural English verb is 'involved'. The answer is A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: Cloze past-participle — pick the past-participle adjective that fits 'A genetic defect is 35_____ in five to ten percent of cases'. The clue is the meaning: defects are PRESENT or PLAY A ROLE in some cases.

steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap
steps.text: 'Though lacking a culprit, experts concur that some mechanism causes brain proteins to misfold, which in turn kills neurons. A genetic defect is 35_____ in five to ten percent of cases, and the aberrations provide interesting clues.'

steps.title: What kind of past-participle does the sentence need?
steps.text: We need a verb that pairs with 'in ___ percent of cases' and means 'present / playing a part'. The standard English collocation for 'plays a part in cases' is 'is involved in'. Other options shift the meaning: 'affected' would imply the genetic defect is something receiving an effect, not contributing one; 'implemented' is for plans / policies; 'caused' would need 'caused BY ___' to read naturally.

steps.title: Vocabulary check (past participles)
steps.text: 'Involved in' = taking part in, present in (a process or set of cases). 'Affected in' = doesn't collocate well; 'affected BY' is the usual form. 'Implemented in' = put into practice in (used for plans, systems, software). 'Caused in' = caused inside; sounds odd because the natural form is 'caused BY' or 'a cause in'.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Walk each option. A 'A genetic defect is involved in five to ten percent of cases' — natural, idiomatic; the genetic defect plays a role in those cases. B 'A genetic defect is affected in… cases' — odd; you don't 'affect a defect'. C 'A genetic defect is implemented in… cases' — wrong register; you implement plans, not defects. D 'A genetic defect is caused in… cases' — confusing; it would mean the defect itself is caused in those cases, when the passage means the defect is one of the causes.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. 'A genetic defect is involved in 5–10% of cases' is the standard medical-journalism collocation for naming a contributing factor.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Affected' is a high-frequency medical word ('patients are affected', 'cases are affected'), so it pattern-matches the gap quickly.
distractors.why_wrong: But 'affected' takes the receiver of an effect, not the giver. A genetic defect contributes to cases, so it is the AGENT, not the RECEIVER. 'Affected by' would mean the defect is shaped by the cases — backwards. Step 4's gloss flags the collocation problem.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Implemented' has a procedural sound that may feel formal enough for a medical text.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Implemented' is for plans, policies, programs — things actively put into practice. A genetic defect is a biological condition, not a plan. Step 5's plug-and-test catches the register mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading hits 'genetic defect' and 'kills neurons' and infers a causal role — 'caused' looks tempting.
distractors.why_wrong: If the defect causes the disease, the natural phrasing is 'A genetic defect CAUSES cases' or 'cases are caused by a genetic defect'. 'Is caused in cases' twists the direction. Step 3's analysis fixes the agent/receiver direction.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-035.technique: On cloze past-participles, ask: is the subject the AGENT (doing) or the RECEIVER (being done to)? 'Involved' works either way; 'affected' and 'caused' have a default direction that often doesn't match the sentence.
host-2013-verb2-ELF-035.pitfall: Collocations rule out otherwise plausible verbs. 'Be involved IN' is so standard for naming a contributing factor that, once you've ruled out the wrong direction, the answer almost picks itself.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The 'Intelligences' passage opens by stating that popular science writers face a dilemma: laypeople bring 'systematic ideas often far removed from scientific ones' and the writer's job is 'to start from these everyday intuitions and move readers towards the scientific concepts', yet they 'often end up simply reinforcing the initial intuitions'. It is hard to explain science to laypeople — that is C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what can be CONCLUDED from the first paragraph. You need the headline of that single paragraph, not a claim from later in the article.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: First paragraph: 'Popular science writers face a dilemma. Laypeople come to science with their own systematic ideas often far removed from scientific ones. To explain science effectively, we need to start from these everyday intuitions and move readers towards the scientific concepts. Yet we often end up simply reinforcing the initial intuitions rather than overturning them.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Laypeople bring everyday ideas about science that don't match the science itself. A popular-science writer has to bridge that gap. But often the writer fails — instead of overturning the wrong everyday ideas, the writing strengthens them. Conclusion: explaining science to laypeople is genuinely hard.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Layperson / laypeople' = non-expert. 'Systematic ideas' = organised, structured ideas (here, the intuitive theories everyday people have). 'Intuitions' = gut-feeling ideas, often wrong but coherent. 'Overturning' = reversing, correcting. 'Far removed from' = very different from.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says popular science writers RELY on intuition — the paragraph says they need to START from laypeople's intuitions, not rely on their own. B says science is most efficient if based on laypeople's ideas — opposite; the paragraph says laypeople's ideas need overturning. C ('It is rather difficult to explain science to laypeople') exactly captures the dilemma the paragraph defines. D says readers' intuitive notions are often CONFIRMED in scientific reports — the paragraph says writers often REINFORCE intuitions despite trying to overturn them, but that is a failure of the writing, not a feature of science itself.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The first paragraph defines a problem (explaining science is hard) and shows why (writers often reinforce the intuitions they wanted to overturn). C names the problem in plain English.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'writers face a dilemma' as 'writers themselves have intuitions to manage', A feels close.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'intuitions' in the paragraph belong to LAYPEOPLE, not to the writers. The writers' job is to manage laypeople's intuitions — they don't rely on their own. Step 2's quotes locate the intuitions firmly with the readers.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'start from these everyday intuitions' and infer that science should be BUILT on laypeople's ideas.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Start from' means 'begin the explanation from', not 'build the science on'. The next sentence makes the goal clear: 'move readers towards the scientific concepts'. Step 3's paraphrase fixes the direction — start there, end somewhere else.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading lands on 'we often end up simply reinforcing the initial intuitions' and might paraphrase that as 'intuitions are confirmed in science reports'.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph criticises this outcome as a failure, not a feature: it happens 'rather than overturning' the intuitions. So D presents the failure as a positive result — the opposite of what the paragraph argues. Step 5 catches the framing flip.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-036.technique: First-paragraph items: the headline is usually the second-to-last sentence (the implicit goal) plus the last sentence (the failure mode). Use both. Here: writers SHOULD overturn intuitions, but OFTEN reinforce them — so explaining is hard.
host-2013-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: When a passage criticises a common failure mode, distractors describe the failure as if it were the desired outcome. Catch the author's evaluative tone ('we often end up simply reinforcing… rather than overturning') — that 'rather than' marks the bad outcome.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: The 'Intelligences' passage warns that popular science 'may even create fabulous intellectual monsters: chimeras of scientific and everyday ideas that take on a life of their own' and that 'the reader's final picture will often be quite different from the one we set out to paint'. Popular science gets reinterpreted through readers' own views — that is B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is SUGGESTED about popular science. 'Suggested' means implied or claimed by the passage as a tendency, not necessarily stated as fact.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two anchors. 'No matter how carefully we report and interpret, the reader's final picture will often be quite different from the one we set out to paint.' And: 'Popular science may even create fabulous intellectual monsters: chimeras of scientific and everyday ideas that take on a life of their own.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: However carefully a science writer explains, the reader ends up with a different mental picture than the writer intended. Sometimes the result is a hybrid of real science and the reader's own ideas — a 'chimera'. So popular science gets bent by each reader's preexisting opinions.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Suggested' = implied, hinted. 'Chimera' (here) = a hybrid creature made from mismatched parts; figuratively, a mixed-up combination of ideas. 'Take on a life of their own' = develop independently, beyond the original control. 'Set out to paint' = intended to depict.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says popular science is FALSIFIED information about social-science research — too strong; the passage says it gets bent by readers, not that it's deliberately falsified. B ('Popular science is interpreted according to people's own opinions') captures 'the reader's final picture will often be quite different from the one we set out to paint' and 'chimeras of scientific and everyday ideas'. C says popular science focuses on people's social and intellectual background — never claimed. D says popular science is common beliefs presented by psychologists — the passage discusses HOW common beliefs distort popular science, not that popular science IS those beliefs.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The paragraph's core suggestion is that readers reshape popular science through their own intuitions and opinions. B names that reshaping directly.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'monsters' and 'chimeras' as 'lies' or 'fakes', A's 'falsified information' feels close.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't accuse popular science of being false — it describes a distortion that happens during READER RECEPTION, not at the writing stage. Step 3's paraphrase makes the chain clear: the writer reports faithfully; the reader twists it. Step 5 catches the falsification overreach.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'beliefs have deep social and political roots and implications' (further down) and infer popular science focuses on demographics.
distractors.why_wrong: That later sentence is about the SUBJECT MATTER (human sciences), not about popular science focusing on social background. Step 2's anchors stay in the first two paragraphs and locate the suggestion about reader interpretation, not focus on demographics.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on the word 'psychologists' (mentioned later in the article) is to read D as the author's framing.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't equate popular science with 'common beliefs presented by psychologists'. The article is BY a psychologist about popular science, but it doesn't define popular science as belief-presentation. Step 5's option-by-option check catches the equation.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-037.technique: 'What is suggested' items: look for hedging language ('often', 'may', 'tends to') and pick the option that captures the same level of claim. Absolute words in distractors ('is falsified') usually overshoot a suggestion.
host-2013-verb2-ELF-037.pitfall: When a passage describes a transformation that happens at the RECEPTION end (reader distortion), distractors blame the SOURCE end (false reporting). Lock onto where in the chain the passage places the problem.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: The 'Intelligences' passage notes that Goleman's and Gardner's books 'come to challenge popular intuitions about intelligence but may end up reinforcing them', and that 'intelligence simply does not play a role in current scientific views of the mind' outside a narrow subfield. The author's note: 'intelligence' has limited scientific applicability — that is A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what the author NOTES in relation to the two books. You need the author's specific evaluative remark about Goleman and Gardner — not just their topic, but the author's commentary.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two anchors. 'For me they are simultaneously attractive and troubling because they come to challenge popular intuitions about 'intelligence' but may end up reinforcing them.' And, more broadly: 'But outside of a narrow subfield within psychometrics, 'intelligence' simply does not play a role in current scientific views of the mind.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The author finds the books attractive AND troubling. They try to challenge the lay idea of intelligence, but probably reinforce it. And the deeper problem: 'intelligence' isn't really a scientific concept outside one narrow subfield. So the books are working with a concept that has limited scientific use.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Note' (verb) = remark, observe. 'Limited applicability' = useful in only a narrow range. 'Psychometrics' = the field that measures mental abilities (IQ tests, etc.). 'Intuition' here = lay theory, gut-feeling idea. 'Reinforcing' = strengthening (here, the popular idea the books wanted to challenge).

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('The notion of intelligence has limited applicability in scientific research') matches 'intelligence simply does not play a role in current scientific views of the mind' outside psychometrics — that is exactly the author's note about why the books are troubling. B says the books present the latest research on intuition and intelligence — partly true for the topic, but not the author's specific note. C says the concept of intelligence is one of today's most important scientific areas — the opposite of the author's claim. D says the books are well written but pay too little attention to psychometrics — the passage praises them as 'effective populari­sers' but doesn't fault them on psychometrics; instead, it questions whether 'intelligence' belongs in serious cognitive science at all.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The author's note in relation to the books is that they centre on a concept ('intelligence') that has very limited scientific role. A captures that exact note.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Both writers are effective populari­sers of science, and both books are clear, informative and readable' and infer the author is summarising the latest research presented in them.
distractors.why_wrong: The author DESCRIBES the books briefly, but his NOTE is the deeper concern about intelligence as a concept. B captures the description, not the note. Step 2's anchors locate the actual concern: the scientific applicability of 'intelligence' itself.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read the books' commercial popularity as evidence the field is important.
distractors.why_wrong: The author argues the opposite: outside one narrow subfield, intelligence doesn't play a role in current cognitive science. Popularity ≠ scientific importance. Step 3's paraphrase fixes the author's position.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'troublesome' and 'troubling' as a critique of how the books handle psychometrics, D feels close.
distractors.why_wrong: The author's complaint isn't about a missing focus on psychometrics — it's about how 'intelligence' as a concept is unscientific OUTSIDE psychometrics. The books' actual technical content is praised. Step 5's option-by-option check catches the wrong angle.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-038.technique: Author-note items: separate the BOOK DESCRIPTION (often complimentary) from the AUTHOR'S NOTE (often a deeper critique). Distractors lift the description; the answer captures the critique.
host-2013-verb2-ELF-038.pitfall: Don't be fooled by an author who calls books 'attractive' and 'effective populari­sers'. The PR-friendly adjectives are often a setup for the deeper objection — read the next sentence with 'but' or 'yet'.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-039.solution_path: The 'Intelligences' passage says Gardner's multiple-intelligences idea was meant to challenge the lay notion of intelligence as one substance, yet 'the very success of the multiple intelligences idea suggests that the audience really does not get it. Rather than seeing that intelligence is not a substance at all, people now seem to think it is several substances'. Gardner's work has ended up STRENGTHENING the everyday concept of intelligence — that is B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what we are told about Gardner's WORK on intelligence — specifically the effect or status of his research, in the author's view.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two anchors. 'Goleman and Gardner recognise this divide between intuitive and scientific ideas. In fact, it was clearly the motivation for Gardner's original work.' And the critical pivot: 'And yet, as Gardner himself sometimes ruefully remarks, the very success of the 'multiple intelligences' idea suggests that the audience really does not get it. Rather than seeing that intelligence is not a substance at all, people now seem to think it is several substances — social, emotional and logico-mathematical.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Gardner originally wanted to challenge the everyday idea of intelligence. But his 'multiple intelligences' framing accidentally backfired: people now believe in MULTIPLE substances of intelligence instead of zero. The lay view that intelligence is a substance has actually grown stronger, not weaker, because of his work.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Ruefully' = with regret. 'Substance' (here) = a stuff-like thing you have a quantity of (like blood sugar). 'Lay concept' = everyday, non-scientific concept. 'Conventional concept of intelligence' = the standard everyday idea that intelligence is a quantity of mental stuff. 'Strengthen' = make stronger, reinforce.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says Gardner found intelligence is ONE single substance — opposite of his theory and of the passage's claim. B ('Gardner's research has turned out to strengthen rather than question the conventional concept of intelligence') matches the passage's claim that his idea reinforced the substance-view by multiplying it. C says Gardner worked with Goleman to develop multiple intelligences — the passage discusses them in parallel, never as collaborators. D says Gardner's work changed the field of psychometrics — the passage says intelligence barely plays a role in current cognitive science outside psychometrics, and doesn't credit Gardner with reshaping it.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. Gardner intended to challenge the conventional concept; the actual outcome strengthened it. B captures that ironic effect of his work.

distractors.why_tempting: If you skim 'intelligence is not a substance at all' as a quote attributed to Gardner, you might invert it and read 'Gardner found intelligence is one substance'.
distractors.why_wrong: That phrase is the AUTHOR'S view of what people should have learned from Gardner, not Gardner's finding. Gardner's actual theory has MULTIPLE intelligences, not one. Step 2's anchor places the 'not a substance' claim with the author. Step 5 catches the misattribution.

distractors.why_tempting: Goleman and Gardner are paired throughout the article, so it's easy to read them as collaborators.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage discusses two SEPARATE books — Goleman's Social Intelligence and Gardner's Multiple Intelligences. They share concerns but didn't co-develop the theory. Step 2's locations are clear: 'Daniel Goleman's Social Intelligence and Howard Gardner's Multiple Intelligences' — two authors, two books.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a successful intellectual idea is to assume it reshaped its field — and psychometrics is the obvious field for intelligence research.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says intelligence barely matters in current cognitive science outside psychometrics, and doesn't credit Gardner with changing psychometrics itself. Step 5's option-by-option check catches the unsupported field-changing claim.

host-2013-verb2-ELF-039.technique: Author-on-author items: when a passage says X 'ruefully remarks' that his own success undermined his goal, the correct option names that ironic backfiring effect. Distractors describe the goal as if it succeeded.
host-2013-verb2-ELF-039.pitfall: Watch for IRONIC outcomes in passages: a thinker who set out to challenge a view ended up strengthening it. The answer names the actual effect (strengthened), not the intended one (challenged).

host-2013-verb2-ELF-040.solution_path: The last paragraph of 'Intelligences' compares 'emotional intelligence' / 'social intelligence' to slogans like 'the nobility of labour' — 'a way of softening the brutal realities of an arbitrary scholastic pecking order'. It is 'more attractive and humane' than The Bell Curve's genetic-scarcity view, 'but it may be just as removed from what Goleman and Gardner intended'. Even well-meaning framings of intelligence remain oversimplified — that is D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what can be CONCLUDED from the LAST paragraph. You need the closing argument's takeaway, not a claim from earlier paragraphs.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: The closing paragraph: 'No wonder people are glad to hear that there is more intelligence around than they thought. Talking about 'emotional intelligence' or 'social intelligence' is like talking about 'the nobility of labour' or 'being a gentleman'.
